discovery.text: From Allan B. Jacobs' Great Streets (1993). One of the world's longest pedestrian streets — Jacobs uses it to explore what happens when you fully commit to walking.

narrative.summary: One of the world's longest pedestrian streets — Jacobs uses it to explore what happens when you fully commit to walking.

blocks.title: Why Jacobs Chose It
blocks.body: 1.1km of continuous pedestrian street through the city centre, varying in width and character from narrow medieval lanes to open squares. Jan Gehl (friend and colleague of Jacobs) did much of the research here. The benchmark for European pedestrianisation.

blocks.title: How to Walk It
blocks.body: Walk the full length. Jacobs is explicit: you have to walk a great street to understand it — the sequence, the proportions, how the edges change. Give it an hour minimum. Sit at a café or on a bench midway. Notice what makes it feel alive.
